summary: My parents both came from Canada and migrated down to the U.S. in 1951 seeking better work opportunities. They were both in the Canadian Air Force before they were discharged and moved here. I have two older siblings born in Canada who later became U.S. citizens. Canada was the starting point for my family's journey before settling in Seattle.

Both my parents served in the Canadian Air Force before they came to the U.S. My dad was an electronic technician in the military. After they were discharged, they decided to move to Seattle in 1951 for better work opportunities. Their military background was a big part of their early lives, but they weren't involved in politics or unions.

My family settled in north Seattle, specifically in Shoreline, after moving from Canada in 1951. I grew up in the Seattle area during the 1950s and 60s, which was a very white, suburban environment. I worked closely with the Port of Seattle for about eight years before joining the ILWU. Seattle has been home to me since childhood and shaped much of my life and work.

I grew up in Shoreline, Washington, a suburb of Seattle, during the 1950s and 60s. It was a very white, Leave-It-to-Beaver type community with little racial diversity. My family lived there with six kids, and it was a good, wholesome upbringing. I attended school there and later Shoreline High School before moving on to college and work.

I attended Shoreline High School in the Seattle area and had a typical public school experience. After graduating, I went on to Shoreline Community College for a couple of years but didn't finish. Shoreline High was where my formal education started before I entered the workforce. It was part of my early life growing up in Shoreline.

After high school, I attended Shoreline Community College for a couple of years but never graduated. I decided to enter the workforce instead of completing college. Shoreline Community College was part of my educational path before I started working at Mitsui O.S.K. Lines and later the Port of Seattle. It was a stepping stone in my early adult years.

Right after college, I was hired by Mitsui O.S.K. Lines and worked there for about four years. My job involved working directly with the Port of Seattle and its personnel. This experience gave me insight into port operations and led to connections like Frank Clark, who encouraged me to apply for a job at the Port of Seattle. Mitsui was my first real job in the maritime industry.

I joined the International Longshore and Warehouse Union in 1980 when they finally opened the doors for women to become longshoremen. I was one of about 12 women who started then, and we set a high standard for women in the union. I was a member of Local 19 for seventeen years, working hard to prove myself despite initial resistance. The ILWU became a big part of my life and identity as a worker.

When I interviewed to become a longshoreman in 1980, a representative from the Pacific Maritime Association was present but didn't say much during the process. The PMA was part of the employer side in the hiring and labor negotiations. Joe Weber, who was with the PMA, was there at my interview, but it was the union rep who questioned me more closely. The PMA was an important organization in the maritime labor environment I worked in.

Frank Clark was one of the bosses at the Port of Seattle when I worked there before joining the union. He encouraged me to apply for a job with the Port, telling me to put in my application. His support helped me transition from working for Mitsui O.S.K. Lines to the Port of Seattle. Frank Clark played a key role in my early career in the maritime industry.

Joe Weber was a representative from the Pacific Maritime Association who was present at my interview when I applied to become a longshoreman in 1980. He didn't say much during the interview, but his presence was notable. The union representative, Bob Lindsey, was more vocal in questioning me. Joe Weber represented the employer side during that pivotal moment in my career.

Bob Lindsey was the union representative who interviewed me when I applied to become a longshoreman in 1980. He asked if I thought I could do the job, and I told him I would give 110 percent. He accepted me after that. I believe Bob Lindsey has since passed away, but he was important in opening the door for women like me in the ILWU.

I worked at Matson, Inc. at one point during my longshore career, where I had an accident that smashed one of my fingers between a cone and a truck tire. It was a painful injury but did not break the finger. This incident taught me to be more careful on the job. Matson was one of the companies I worked for during my time as a longshoreman.

The transcript cuts off before I could fully discuss the 2002 West Coast longshore lockout, but it was a significant event in the industry during my career. I was active in the ILWU during that period, and such labor disputes affected all of us longshore workers. The lockout was part of the ongoing struggle between unions and employers on the West Coast maritime docks.
